english: Versicherte (insured person) + Karte (card).\nBring it to every doctor visit.

A: Do you have your insurance card with you?
B: Yes, here you go.
A: Thank you. Please take a seat in the waiting room.
B: How long do I have to wait?
From 'zahlen' (to pay) + -ung.\n'Monatliche Zahlung' = monthly payment.

A: The payment is due.
B: When do I have to pay?
A: By the 15th of the month.
B: Can I pay by bank transfer?
From 'mahnen' (to warn/remind).\nThree levels: polite, firm, legal action.

A: I received a payment reminder!
B: The first or the second one?
A: The second one.
B: Then you need to pay quickly.
Aus (out) + Gabe (giving). Money going out.\nOpposite of Einnahme (income).

A: How high are your monthly expenses?
B: About 1500 euros.
A: Those are high expenses.
B: Yes, the rent alone costs 800 euros.
From 'erhöhen' (to increase/raise).\nCompound with everything: Beitrags-, Miet-, Lohnerhöhung.

A: There is a premium increase.
B: Again? By how much?
A: By 0.5 percent.
B: That is just a small increase.
From Latin 'summa.' International, mathematical.\n'In Summe' = in total.

A: How high is the total?
B: The total amounts to 3,500 euros.
A: That is a large sum.
B: Yes, but it includes everything.
'Einkommensteuer' = income tax.\n'Mehrwertsteuer' = VAT. Germany's tax system is famously complex.

A: How much tax do you pay?
B: Way too much! Almost 40 percent.
A: The tax in Germany is high.
B: Yes, but the system works because of it.
'Mindestlohn' = minimum wage.\nDifferent from 'Gehalt' (monthly salary, white-collar).

A: How high is the minimum wage?
B: 12.41 euros per hour.
A: Is the wage enough to live on?
B: In Berlin it is tight.
Be- + rechnen (to calculate).\nCalculation and billing in one verb.

A: Can you calculate the costs?
B: Yes, I will calculate everything for you.
A: What do you charge for the examination?
B: We charge that through the health insurance.
From French 'financer.' The -ieren ending = borrowed verb.\nRegular, predictable, international.

A: How do you finance your studies?
B: My parents finance it partly.
A: And the rest?
B: I finance that with a part-time job.
Ein (in) + Nahme (taking). Money coming in.\nAlso: 'Einnahme von Medizin' = taking medicine.

A: What is your monthly income?
B: My income is 2,500 euros net.
A: And the expenses?
B: About 1,800 euros.
Ein (in) + Kauf (purchase).\n'Einkäufe machen' = to do shopping.

A: I still need to do shopping.
B: What do you need?
A: Just the daily shopping. Bread, milk, vegetables.
B: Should I come along?
From 'kaufen' (to buy).\n'Kaufvertrag' = purchase contract. Short, sharp, commercial.

A: Was that a good purchase?
B: Yes, a very good purchase.
A: How much did you pay?
B: Only 50 euros. A real bargain.
From 'gewinnen' (to win).\n'Gewinn machen' = to make a profit.

A: Did the company make a profit?
B: Yes, the profit was higher than last year.
A: How high was the profit?
B: Almost one million euros.
From 'nieder' (down).\n'Niedrig' for measurable things, 'tief' for depth and emotion.

A: The costs are relatively low.
B: Really? How low?
A: Only 150 euros per month.
B: That is indeed low.
More formal than 'niedrig.'\n'Nicht im Geringsten' = not in the slightest.

A: Are the risks minor?
B: Yes, only a minor risk.
A: Then I will do it.
B: The costs are also minor.
'Knapp bei Kasse' = tight on money.\n'Knapp gewonnen' = barely won.

A: Do you have enough money?
B: It is tight. Very tight.
A: Is it enough for the insurance?
B: Just barely. Tight, but it is enough.

In German, prepositions stay with the relative pronoun. In English, you might say 'the letter I am complaining ABOUT.' In German, the preposition comes FIRST:

Der Brief, ÜBER DEN ich mich beschwere.

The preposition opens the relative clause, followed by the relative pronoun, and the verb goes to the end.

The relative pronoun matches:
- Gender from the noun it refers to
- Case from the preposition

Examples:
- Die Karte, MIT DER du zum Arzt gehst (mit takes dative, die is feminine)
- Der Brief, ÜBER DEN du dich beschwerst (über takes accusative, der is masculine)
- Das Formular, AUF DAS ich warte (auf takes accusative, das is neuter)

Step 1: Identify the preposition the verb needs (sich beschweren ÜBER, warten AUF, sich entscheiden FÜR).

Step 2: Determine the case the preposition requires (accusative or dative).

Step 3: Match the relative pronoun to the gender of the noun AND the case from the preposition.

Der Brief (über + acc, masculine) = über den
Die Karte (mit + dat, feminine) = mit der
Das Formular (auf + acc, neuter) = auf das

The verb always goes to the end of the relative clause.
From Latin 'emplastrum.'\nAlso means 'pavement' (Kopfsteinpflaster = cobblestone).

A: Do you have a bandage?
B: Yes, here. Large or small?
A: A large bandage please.
B: Here, stick it on the wound.
Cognate with English 'salve.'\nFrom Latin 'salvare' (to save/heal).

A: This ointment helps against pain.
B: How often should I apply the ointment?
A: Twice a day.
B: Morning and evening?
Cognate with English 'salve.' From Latin 'salvare' (to save/heal). One of the oldest medicine words in Germanic languages. Apply to Haut for relief.
From Latin 'pilula' (little ball).\n'Die Pille' alone usually means the contraceptive pill.

A: Take these pills three times daily.
B: Before or after eating?
A: After eating. With water.
B: How long should I take them?
'Die Pille' by itself usually means the contraceptive pill. For other pills: 'Tablette' or 'Pille gegen Kopfschmerzen.' Context-dependent social meaning.
From 'spritzen' (to spray/squirt).\n'Spritzenphobie' is a real diagnosis.

A: I need an injection for the pain.
B: Are you afraid of injections?
A: A little.
B: Don't worry, it only takes a second.
Germans fear the Spritze more than most medical procedures. 'Spritzenphobie' is a real diagnosis. From 'spritzen' (to spray/squirt).
'Augentropfen' = eye drops, 'Nasentropfen' = nose drops.\nAlso the verb 'tropfen' (to drip).

A: Take these drops three times a day.
B: How many drops?
A: Ten drops in a glass of water.
B: Before or after eating?
'Ein Tropfen auf den heißen Stein' (a drop on a hot stone) means a futile effort. The drop evaporates before it can help. A beautifully pessimistic idiom.
Schmerz (pain) + Mittel (means/remedy).\nTransparent compound: pain-means.

A: Do you have a painkiller?
B: Yes, ibuprofen or paracetamol?
A: What do you recommend?
B: For headaches: ibuprofen.
German compounds are transparent: pain-means. You always know what you are swallowing. 'Schmerzmittel einnehmen' (to take painkillers).
From 'schnupfen' (to sniff).\nLess formal than Erkältung. Focuses on the nose.

A: Do you have a cold?
B: Yes, my nose is running all day.
A: Here, take a tissue.
B: Thanks. This cold won't go away.
Schnupfen focuses on the nose; Erkältung is the whole-body cold. From 'schnupfen' (to sniff). Less formal, more specific.
Also the verb 'husten' (to cough).\n'Hustensirup' = cough syrup. Onomatopoeia.

A: How long have you had a cough?
B: For a week.
A: Dry cough or with mucus?
B: Dry cough, especially at night.
Onomatopoeia: the word sounds like a cough. Same root as English 'cough' before English changed its spelling. 'Hustensirup' (cough syrup) is a pharmacy staple.
From Latin 'infectio.'\nThe -tion ending is always feminine, always stressed on the last syllable.

A: It is a bacterial infection.
B: Do I need antibiotics?
A: Yes, I will prescribe some.
B: How long does the infection last?
Identical in most European languages. The -tion ending is always feminine in German, always stressed on the last syllable. A reliable pattern.
From Latin 'virus' (poison).\nNeuter (das Virus), though some say 'der Virus.'

A: Is it a virus or an infection?
B: A virus. Antibiotics won't help.
A: What should I do?
B: Rest, fluids, and wait it out.
Neuter in German (das Virus), though some Germans say 'der Virus.' The Duden accepts both. Scientists insist on 'das.' A rare grammar debate in medicine.
Ver- + schreiben (to write). The doctor writes for you.\n'Sich verschreiben' = to make a writing mistake.

A: The doctor prescribed me drops.
B: For what?
A: For the infection.
B: Good, take them regularly.
The doctor writes something for you. 'Sich verschreiben' means to make a writing mistake. The ver- prefix changes everything. One letter, two very different meanings.
Ein + nehmen. Separable prefix.\n'Nehmen Sie die Tabletten zweimal täglich ein.'

A: How should I take the medicine?
B: Twice daily, morning and evening.
A: Before or after eating?
B: Take it after eating.
Separable: 'Nehmen Sie die Tabletten zweimal täglich ein.' Also: 'Einnahmen' (revenue). Medicine intake and money intake share a verb.
From Latin 'operari.' Regular: -ieren ending.\n'Am Knie operiert' = operated on the knee.

A: We need to operate.
B: Is the operation dangerous?
A: No, a routine procedure.
B: When will you operate?
The -ieren ending makes it regular and predictable. 'Operiert werden' (to be operated on). A serious verb for a serious procedure.
Feminine of Arzt. The -in suffix is standard.\nModern: 'Ärzt*in' for inclusive forms.

A: The doctor will be right with you.
B: Which doctor?
A: Dr. Weber. She is very good.
B: Good, I will wait.
The feminine of Arzt. In modern German, many institutions use 'Ärzt*in' to be inclusive. The -in suffix is the standard feminine marker for person nouns.
Feminine of Doktor.\n'Frau Doktor' is more common than 'Frau Doktorin.'

A: Good day, Doctor.
B: Good day. What can I do for you?
A: I have a stomachache.
B: Since when?
'Frau Doktorin Müller' is extremely formal. Most people simply say 'Frau Doktor.' The -in is grammatically correct but socially optional with titles.
From 'schwierig' (difficult) + -keit.\nAbstract noun from adjective.

A: Do you have difficulties with the form?
B: Yes, I do not understand point 5.
A: That is a known difficulty.
B: Can someone help me?
The -keit suffix turns adjectives into abstract nouns. 'Finanzielle Schwierigkeiten' (financial difficulties). The insurance letters are full of Schwierigkeiten.
From Latin 'maximus.'\n'Maximal zwei Tabletten pro Tag.'

A: How many tablets am I allowed to take?
B: Maximum three per day.
A: And if the pain gets worse?
B: Then come back.
In insurance: 'maximale Erstattung' (maximum reimbursement). International, transparent, precise. From Latin 'maximus.'
The opposite of maximal.\n'Minimaler Aufwand, maximaler Effekt.'

A: How big is the risk?
B: Minimal. Really very low.
A: Are there minimal side effects?
B: Yes, only minimal.
'Minimaler Aufwand, maximaler Effekt' (minimal effort, maximum effect). Insurance companies love this phrase. For them, not for you.
Des (of that) + wegen (because of). Synonym of deshalb.\nMain clause connector: verb in second position after it.

A: Verumius was sick. Therefore he went to the doctor.
B: Did the doctor prescribe something?
A: Yes, therefore he feels better.
B: Good, therefore he can come back tomorrow.
Deswegen, deshalb, daher, darum all mean therefore. Germans use them interchangeably. Deswegen literally breaks down to des (of that) + wegen (because of): because of that.

These words connect two main clauses and show cause, contrast, or consequence. The verb comes IMMEDIATELY after them (V2 position).

The pattern is: first main clause, then the adverbial conjunction, then the verb, then the subject.

- deshalb (therefore): Er ist krank. Deshalb geht er zum Arzt.
- trotzdem (nevertheless): Es regnet. Trotzdem geht sie joggen.
- deswegen (that is why): Die Kosten steigen. Deswegen zahlen wir mehr.
- also (so/therefore): Du bist versichert. Also musst du dir keine Sorgen machen.
- sonst (otherwise): Nimm die Medizin. Sonst wird es schlimmer.

Compare with weil (subordinating), which pushes the verb to the END:
Er geht zum Arzt, weil er krank ist.

Deshalb (adverbial) keeps the verb in second position:
Er ist krank. Deshalb GEHT er zum Arzt.

Subordinating conjunctions (weil, dass, obwohl, damit, falls) push the verb to the END of the clause:
Er geht zum Arzt, weil er krank IST.

Adverbial conjunctions (deshalb, trotzdem, deswegen, also, sonst) keep the verb in SECOND position:
Er ist krank. Deshalb GEHT er zum Arzt.

The key difference: subordinating conjunctions create Nebensätze (subordinate clauses). Adverbial conjunctions connect two Hauptsätze (main clauses). The word order tells you which type you are dealing with.

Both trigger inversion (verb in position 2), but with opposite logic:

deshalb = expected result (consequence):
Es regnet. Deshalb bleibe ich zu Hause. (logical)

trotzdem = unexpected result (surprise):
Es regnet. Trotzdem gehe ich raus. (unexpected)

deshalb = 'deswegen' / trotzdem = 'trotz dessen' (despite that)

trotzdem = everyday speech
dennoch = formal written language, essays, news

'Der Markt fiel. Dennoch blieben die Anleger ruhig.'
(The market fell. Nevertheless the investors remained calm.)

In conversation: trotzdem.
In an essay: dennoch.

obwohl = subordinating conjunction, verb goes to the END:
Obwohl es regnet, gehe ich raus.
[Subclause: verb LAST] [Main clause: V2]

trotzdem = adverb, verb stays in position 2:
Es regnet. Trotzdem gehe ich raus.
[Main clause 1] [Main clause 2]

Same meaning. Completely different grammar.
Test: Can you start a new sentence with it? trotzdem YES, obwohl NO.

With obwohl (one complex sentence):
Obwohl es kalt ist, gehe ich schwimmen.
[Subclause: verb at end] [Main clause: V2]

With trotzdem (two main clauses):
Es ist kalt. Trotzdem gehe ich schwimmen.
[Main clause 1] [Main clause 2: V2 after trotzdem]

Practice converting:
obwohl sentence to trotzdem sentence and vice versa.

From casual to formal:

auch = also (simplest form)
außerdem = moreover (everyday, versatile)
zudem = furthermore (slightly formal)
darüber hinaus = beyond that (essays only)

All add new information. außerdem is the safe default.

Position 1 (with inversion):
Außerdem spricht sie Französisch.

Middle of sentence (without inversion):
Sie spricht außerdem Französisch.

Both correct. Position 1 emphasizes the addition.

sowohl A als auch B = both A and B:
Sie ist sowohl intelligent als auch fleißig.

weder A noch B = neither A nor B:
Er trinkt weder Kaffee noch Tee.

IMPORTANT: weder...noch is already negative.
Never add nicht or kein on top:
Er trinkt weder Kaffee noch Tee. (correct)
Er trinkt nicht weder Kaffee noch Tee. (incorrect)

With full sentences, weder triggers inversion in both parts:
Weder hat er Zeit, noch hat er Lust.
(Neither does he have time, nor does he feel like it.)

The verb comes in BOTH parts directly after weder/noch.
After an adverbial conjunction, the verb comes immediately next.
Bei takes dative. Ärztin is feminine. What is the feminine dative relative pronoun?
The doctor I was at prescribed me drops.
Which conjunction shows a direct cause-effect relationship?
First blank: in + dative, feminine Klinik. Second blank: a consequence conjunction.
The clinic I was at was very good. Therefore I recommend it.
NOT a drugstore in the American sense.\ndm, Rossmann, Müller: toiletries, cosmetics, household. No medicine.

A: Where do you buy shampoo?
B: At the drugstore. At dm or Rossmann.
A: Not at the pharmacy?
B: No, the drugstore is for personal care.
A famous false friend. A Drogerie sells toiletries, cosmetics, and household products. No prescription drugs. For medicine, you need the Apotheke.
Zahn (tooth) + Creme (cream).\nAlso called 'Zahnpasta.' Both are correct.

A: Which toothpaste do you use?
B: One with fluoride.
A: Where do you buy it?
B: At the drugstore. It is cheaper there.
Germans use both Zahncreme and Zahnpasta interchangeably. Regional preference: north tends toward Zahnpasta, south toward Zahncreme.
Zahn (tooth) + Pasta (paste). From Italian.\nSynonym for Zahncreme.

A: Do you still have toothpaste?
B: No, it is empty.
A: I will buy new toothpaste.
B: Get the one with herbs.
From Italian 'pasta' (paste). The other word for toothpaste. Both Zahncreme and Zahnpasta are correct. Regional and personal preference decides.
Zahn (tooth) + Bürste (brush).\nCompound noun: you already know both halves.

A: I need a new toothbrush.
B: An electric one or a normal one?
A: An electric toothbrush.
B: Those cost from 30 euros.
Compound noun: you already know both halves. This is how German vocabulary grows exponentially. Know the parts, know the whole.
Not just for teeth: Haarbürste, Schuhbürste, Nagelbürste.\nA bristle tool for cleaning anything.

A: Where is my brush?
B: Which brush? The hairbrush?
A: Yes, the hairbrush.
B: In the bathroom, next to the mirror.
Haarbürste (hairbrush), Schuhbürste (shoe brush), Nagelbürste (nail brush). A bristle tool for cleaning anything. From Middle High German 'burste.'
Tasche (pocket) + Tuch (cloth).\nTempo is the generic German word for tissue.

A: Do you have a tissue?
B: Yes, here. Do you have a cold?
A: Yes, since yesterday.
B: Get well soon!
Tempo is the generic German word for tissue (brand name, like Kleenex in English). 'Hast du ein Taschentuch?' is what you ask when someone has Schnupfen.
From 'pflegen' (to care for) + -er.\n'Altenpfleger' = elderly care worker.

A: The caregiver comes at eight o'clock.
B: Is he nice?
A: Yes, a very good caregiver.
B: Good, grandma needs good care.
An increasingly important profession in aging Germany. The country has a chronic shortage of Pfleger. 'Altenpfleger' (elderly care worker) is one of the most needed jobs.
Pfleger + -in. Standard feminine suffix.\nThe majority of Pflege workers are female.

A: The caregiver is very experienced.
B: How long has she been working?
A: For ten years as a caregiver.
B: You can tell.
The majority of Pflege workers in Germany are female. The profession is chronically understaffed. Same pattern: male -er, female -erin.
Kranke (sick person) + Pfleger (carer).\nModern: 'Pflegefachkraft' for all genders.

A: My brother is a nurse.
B: At which clinic does he work?
A: At the Charité.
B: That is a very good profession.
Modern German is moving toward 'Pflegefachkraft' for all genders. But 'Krankenpfleger' is still widely understood and used.
Kranke (sick) + Schwester (sister).\nHistorically, nuns cared for the sick. The name stuck.

A: The nurse is measuring my blood pressure.
B: Is it too high?
A: No, says the nurse.
B: Good, then everything is fine.
Historically, nuns (Schwestern) cared for the sick. The name stuck. Being phased out officially in favor of 'Pflegefachfrau,' but still widely used.
From 'betreuen' (to look after).\nBroader than Pfleger: children, patients, elderly.

A: Who is the supervisor of the group?
B: Mr. Schmidt. He looks after ten patients.
A: Is he good?
B: Yes, a very caring supervisor.
Broader than Pfleger: a Betreuer can supervise children, patients, or elderly. 'Kinderbetreuer' (childcare worker). The person responsible for your wellbeing.
Betreuer + -in.\n'Die Betreuerin kümmert sich um die Senioren.'

A: The caretaker comes this afternoon.
B: Good, grandpa always looks forward to it.
A: Yes, the caretaker is very nice.
B: He trusts her.
Same pattern: male -er, female -erin. Betreuer/Betreuerin, Pfleger/Pflegerin. German is consistent with its gendered suffixes.
From 'betreuen' + -ung. Abstract noun.\n'Kinderbetreuung' = childcare.

A: The care at the nursing home is very good.
B: How many hours per day?
A: 24-hour care.
B: That is reassuring.
'Kinderbetreuung' (childcare), 'Seniorenbetreuung' (elderly care). The -ung suffix creates the concept from the verb. German's favorite noun-maker.
'Einen Kranken pflegen' = to care for a sick person.\nAlso: 'Kontakte pflegen' = to maintain contacts.

A: Who cares for your mother?
B: A caregiver comes every day.
A: Does he care for her well?
B: Yes, he cares for her with great patience.
Care in every sense. 'Einen Kranken pflegen' (to care for a sick person). 'Kontakte pflegen' (to maintain contacts). 'Den Garten pflegen' (to tend the garden).
Inseparable prefix be-.\nBroader than pflegen. 'Kinder betreuen' = to look after children.

A: Who looks after the children?
B: A caretaker looks after them.
A: How many children does she look after?
B: Ten children per group.
Be- + treuen (loyalty-related root). To be loyal to someone by watching over them. Inseparable prefix. Broader than pflegen.
Er- + leicht (light/easy) + -ern. To make lighter.\n'Erleichtert sein' = to be relieved.

A: This medicine eases the pain.
B: Really? How fast?
A: After about 30 minutes.
B: Good, I am relieved.
A beautiful compound verb. Er- + leicht (light/easy) + -ern. To make lighter. Recovery is literally making the burden lighter.
Ver- + binden (to bind).\n'Eine Wunde verbinden' = to bandage a wound.

A: I need to bandage the wound.
B: Do you have a bandage?
A: Yes, I will bandage it right away.
B: Thanks, that feels better already.
The bandage binds; the phone line connects. Same verb. 'Eine Wunde verbinden' (to bandage a wound). 'Verbunden sein' (to be connected).
Er- + setzen (to set/place).\nTo set something new in the place of the old.

A: We need to replace the bandage.
B: How often?
A: Replace it every day.
B: And then the wound heals faster?
'Nichts kann Gesundheit ersetzen' (Nothing can replace health). To set something new in the place of the old. The verb that built the Ersatz noun.
From ersetzen. 'Ersatzteil' = spare part.\nEntered English during the World Wars.

A: Do you have a substitute for this medicine?
B: Yes, there is a cheaper substitute.
A: Does the substitute work just as well?
B: Yes, same active ingredient.
During both World Wars, 'Ersatz' entered English as a word meaning cheap substitute. Germans use it neutrally. 'Ersatzteil' (spare part). 'Kaffee-Ersatz' (coffee substitute).
From 'besser' (better) + -ung.\n'Gute Besserung!' = Get well soon!

A: Get well soon!
B: Thanks! I hope for a quick recovery.
A: Rest well.
B: I will. Thanks for the well wishes.
'Gute Besserung!' is THE thing Germans say when someone is sick. The most important two-word phrase in German healthcare culture. Say it often.
This store sells toiletries and cosmetics, not prescription medicine.
Which word means 'therefore' and is followed by verb-second word order?
The nurse works hard. Therefore she is often exhausted.
First blank: accusative relative pronoun for feminine Salbe. Second blank: consequence conjunction + verb-second.
The ointment the doctor prescribed me does not help. That is why I need a different painkiller.
'In Rente gehen' = to retire. From Latin 'renta.'\nRentenversicherung (pension insurance) is mandatory.

A: When will you retire?
B: At 67. Another 30 years.
A: Will you get a good pension?
B: I hope so. I pay in every month.
The German Rentensystem is pay-as-you-go: today's workers fund today's retirees. 'Rentenversicherung' (pension insurance) is mandatory for all employees.
Two meanings: retirement pay for Beamte, or a small hotel.\nContext is essential.

A: My father gets a pension.
B: Is he a civil servant?
A: Yes, he was a teacher for 40 years.
B: Then the pension is surely good.
Two meanings. For civil servants: retirement pay (Beamtenpension). For travelers: a small guesthouse. 'In Pension gehen' (to retire, for Beamte). Context is essential.
From Latin 'senior' (older).\n'Seniorenrabatt' = senior discount.

A: Is there a senior discount?
B: Yes, for seniors from 65 years.
A: How many percent?
B: 20 percent on all tickets.
'Seniorenrabatt' (senior discount), 'Seniorenticket' (reduced fare). Respectful term for older adults. From Latin 'senior' (older).
Senior + -in. Standard feminine formation.\nGerman insists on gendered forms, even for international words.

A: The senior woman jogs every morning.
B: How old is she?
A: 78 years old.
B: Impressive! An athletic senior.
German insists on gendered forms, even for international words. Senior becomes Seniorin. The -in suffix is universal for person nouns.
Austrian German primarily.\nIn Germany, 'Rentner' is more common.

A: My neighbor is a pensioner.
B: From Austria?
A: Yes, he is from Vienna.
B: There they say Pensionist instead of Rentner.
Austrian German primarily. In Germany, 'Rentner' is more common. Regional variation within German-speaking countries shows the language is not monolithic.
Austrian form. Pensionist + -in.\nStandard feminine formation.

A: The pensioner lives in Salzburg.
B: Does she get a good pension?
A: Yes, she was a teacher.
B: Teachers get good pensions.
Austrian form. Four morphemes: Pension-ist-in. Standard feminine formation applied to a regional word.
Alten (old people) + Heim (home).\nGermany has over 15,000 Altenheime.

A: My grandma lives in the nursing home.
B: Does she like it there?
A: Yes, the nursing home is very nice.
B: Does she have good care?
A place where Senioren receive Betreuung and Pflege. Germany has over 15,000 Altenheime. The aging population makes this an increasingly important topic.
Alter (age) + Heim (home). Synonym for Altenheim.\nRegional preference decides which one you hear.

A: Is there a retirement home nearby?
B: Yes, on the outskirts.
A: Is the retirement home good?
B: Yes, it has a good reputation.
'Altersheim' focuses on age; 'Altenheim' focuses on the people. Both mean the same thing. Regional preference decides which one you hear.
Ein (in) + Richtung (direction) = arrangement.\nAlso means furniture ('die Einrichtung des Zimmers').

A: The facility is modern.
B: Which facility do you mean?
A: The medical facility by the park.
B: Ah yes, that one only opened last year.
To set things in the right direction. 'Eine medizinische Einrichtung' (a medical facility). Also: furniture ('die Einrichtung des Zimmers'). Direction and arrangement in one word.
The -ieren ending makes it regular.\nPrimarily for Beamte. Workers: 'in Rente gehen.'

A: When will he be retired?
B: Next year.
A: Is he looking forward to it?
B: Yes, he was informed last month.
Primarily for civil servants (Beamte). For normal workers: 'in Rente gehen.' Germany has two retirement systems: one for Beamte, one for everyone else.
Root of Notfall, Notaufnahme, Notruf, Notausgang.\nOne tiny word spawns an entire emergency vocabulary.

A: He is in great distress.
B: What happened?
A: He lost his money.
B: We need to help him.
Root of Notfall, Notaufnahme, Notruf, Notausgang. One tiny word spawns an entire emergency vocabulary. 'Not macht erfinderisch' (Necessity is the mother of invention).
Not (emergency) + Ausgang (exit).\nGreen signs, required by law in every public building.

A: Where is the emergency exit?
B: Back there, next to the stairs.
A: Thanks. You should always know where it is.
B: Exactly, that is safety.
Green signs with a running figure. Required by law in every public building. 'Wo ist der Notausgang?' is essential survival German.
From Latin 'offerre' (to offer).\nBoth meanings: the one harmed and the one given up.

A: How many victims were there?
B: Three injured, no dead.
A: That is terrible.
B: Yes, the victims are being treated in the hospital.
The word holds both meanings: the one harmed and the one given up. 'Unfallopfer' (accident victim). 'Ein Opfer bringen' (to make a sacrifice). Same word, dual fate.
Masculine despite being abstract.\nCognate with English 'death.' Often personified.

A: Death is a difficult topic.
B: Yes, but an important one.
A: In Germany, people talk about it openly.
B: I think that is good.
Often personified in German literature as 'der Sensenmann' (the grim reaper). Cognate with English 'death.' Masculine, despite being an abstract noun.
Adjectival noun from 'tot' (dead).\nDer/die Tote changes with gender and case like an adjective.

A: The police found a dead woman.
B: Who was the dead woman?
A: That is still being investigated.
B: The number of dead is rising.
Adjectival noun: der/die Tote changes with gender and case like an adjective. 'Die Zahl der Toten' (the number of dead). Grammatically, death is declined.
Adjectival noun from 'krank.'\nDer Kranke (male), die Kranke (female).

A: The sick woman needs rest.
B: How is the sick woman doing?
A: Better. The sick woman is recovering slowly.
B: That is good news.
Another adjective acting as a noun. Der Kranke (male), die Kranke (female). 'Die Kranken pflegen' (to care for the sick). Adjective becomes person.
From Tod + -lich.\n'Tödlich langweilig' = deadly boring (emphasis).

A: Is the disease deadly?
B: No, not with the right treatment.
A: Thank God.
B: But without treatment it can be deadly.
Germans use tödlich for emphasis, like English 'dead tired.' 'Tödlich langweilig' (deadly boring). From Tod + -lich. The adjective of death.
From 'liefern' (to deliver) + -ung.\nIn insurance: what the Krankenkasse delivers (pays for).

A: When does the delivery come?
B: The delivery comes tomorrow.
A: Good, I am waiting for the medicine delivery.
B: It will be delivered by 12 o'clock.
In the insurance context: what the Krankenkasse delivers (pays for). 'Medikamentenlieferung' (medicine delivery). From 'liefern' (to deliver) + -ung.
sich entscheiden für + accusative. Versicherung is feminine.
The separable verb for making regular contributions to a pension or insurance fund.
Every employee must pay into the pension insurance monthly.
From 'rauchen' (to smoke) + -er.\n'Raucherbereich' = smoking area.

A: Are you a smoker?
B: No, I quit three years ago.
A: Well done! Smoking is unhealthy.
B: Yes, I feel much better.
Germany was one of the last EU countries to ban indoor smoking (2007). 'Raucher oder Nichtraucher?' was once asked on every train.
Raucher + -in. Standard feminine suffix.\nSame pattern as Pfleger/Pflegerin.

A: Is she a smoker?
B: Yes, unfortunately. She has been smoking for 20 years.
A: That is bad for health.
B: I know, but she cannot quit.
Same pattern as Pfleger/Pflegerin, Betreuer/Betreuerin. German's consistent feminine suffix at work.
Nicht + Raucher. The Nicht- prefix negates.\n'Nichtraucherzone' = non-smoking zone.

A: I am a non-smoker.
B: Very good. Since always?
A: Yes, I have never smoked.
B: Enviable!
The Nicht- prefix negates the person noun. 'Nichtraucher sein' (to be a non-smoker) is now the default in public spaces across Germany.
Nichtraucher + -in.\nFour morphemes: Nicht-rauch-er-in.

A: She is a committed non-smoker.
B: Good for her.
A: Yes, she never started.
B: That is the best decision.
Four morphemes in one word: Nicht-rauch-er-in. German stacks prefixes and suffixes like building blocks. Know the parts, decode any word.
From 'suchen' (to seek). An endless seeking.\n'Drogensucht' = drug addiction.

A: Addiction is a disease.
B: Yes, a very serious one.
A: Can you cure addiction?
B: With therapy and support, yes.
From 'suchen' (to seek). An addiction is an endless seeking. 'Drogensucht' (drug addiction), 'Spielsucht' (gambling addiction), 'Internetsucht' (internet addiction). Whatever you cannot stop seeking.
Sucht (addiction) + Mittel (substance/means).\nClinical, official, legal.

A: Alcohol is an addictive substance.
B: Yes, and nicotine too.
A: Which addictive substances are legal?
B: Alcohol, nicotine, and caffeine.
The compound is clinical, official, legal. Used in medical and legal contexts. 'Suchtmittelgesetz' (narcotics law). German compounds leave nothing ambiguous.
From Dutch 'droog' (dry). Originally dried herbs.\nThe Drogerie kept the old, innocent meaning.

A: Drugs are dangerous.
B: Yes, especially hard drugs.
A: Is there help for addicts?
B: Yes, there are drug counseling centers.
From Dutch 'droog' (dry). Originally meant dried herbs and spices. The meaning shifted to illegal substances. The Drogerie kept the old, innocent meaning.
The ultimate false friend.\nEnglish 'gift' = present. German 'Gift' = poison.

A: Careful, that is poison!
B: Really? It looks harmless.
A: No, that is very poisonous material.
B: Thanks for the warning!
The ultimate false friend. English 'gift' = present. German 'Gift' = poison. Same root (something given), opposite outcomes. Do not confuse at the pharmacy.
From Sucht + -ig.\n'Süchtig machen' = to be addictive. Can be serious or playful.

A: Are you addicted to coffee?
B: Yes, totally addicted! Three cups a day.
A: That is not so bad yet.
B: But without coffee I cannot function.
Can be serious or playful. 'Süchtig nach Schokolade' (addicted to chocolate) is lighthearted. 'Drogenabhängig' is the clinical term for serious addiction.
Same in English and German.\n'Blinder Passagier' = stowaway (blind passenger).

A: My grandpa is almost blind.
B: Does he need help in daily life?
A: Yes, a caregiver comes daily.
B: Good that he has support.
From Proto-Germanic *blindaz. 'Blinder Passagier' (stowaway, literally 'blind passenger'). 'Blindenschrift' = Braille (blind-writing).
Cognate with English 'deaf' through sound shifts.\nAlso means 'numb': 'Mein Finger ist taub.'

A: She has been deaf since childhood.
B: Can she do sign language?
A: Yes, fluently.
B: Impressive.
Also means 'numb.' 'Mein Finger ist taub' (My finger is numb). 'Gehörlos' (hearing-less) is preferred in modern usage over 'taubstumm' (deaf-mute).
'Die linke Hand' = the left hand.\nColloquially: 'ein linker Typ' = a shady character.

A: Are you left-handed?
B: Yes, I write with the left hand.
A: Interesting. My brother too.
B: We are about ten percent of the population.
The association of left with dishonesty exists in many languages (Latin 'sinister'). Colloquially: 'ein linker Typ' (a shady character). An ancient bias preserved in language.
Irregular: sticht, stach, gestochen.\nRelated to English 'stick.'

A: Ow! The bee stung me!
B: Where did it sting you?
A: On the arm. It burns and stings.
B: Let me see. Are you allergic?
'Die Biene sticht' (The bee stings). 'Es sticht in der Brust' (There is a stabbing pain in the chest). Related to English 'stick.' Irregular: sticht, stach, gestochen.
Irregular: schob, geschoben.\n'Den Rollstuhl schieben' = to push the wheelchair.

A: Can you push the wheelchair?
B: Yes, no problem.
A: Carefully, the ramp is steep.
B: I will push very slowly.
'Etwas auf die lange Bank schieben' (to put something on the long bench) = to procrastinate. Irregular: schob, geschoben. Pushing in every sense.
Irregular: wog, gewogen.\nAlso: 'das Baby wiegen' = to rock the baby.

A: How much do you weigh?
B: About 80 kilos.
A: That is a normal weight.
B: Yes, I pay attention to my health.
Two different verbs that merged into one form. 'Wie viel wiegen Sie?' (How much do you weigh?). 'Das Baby wiegen' (to rock the baby). Context decides.
This is the most famous German-English false friend. Not a present!
Which preposition means 'at' or 'from' when collecting something from a place?
The pharmacy I get my medicine from is nearby.
First blank: dative masculine relative pronoun. Second blank: contrast conjunction (despite that).
The patient the doctor gave the injection to was afraid. Nevertheless, he was relieved afterwards.
This conjunction shows direct consequence, similar to 'deshalb.'
Which conjunction means 'otherwise' and warns of a negative consequence?
Take your medicine. Otherwise it will get worse.
From Latin 'moneta' (mint).\nRelated to English 'money' and 'mint.'

A: Do you have a coin for the machine?
B: Yes, here is a 1-euro coin.
A: Thanks! The machine only takes coins.
B: No bills? Only coins?
German parking meters still love Münzen. The 1-Euro-Münze has country-specific designs on the back. Each country's coin tells a different story.
Separable: 'Geld abheben' = to withdraw money.\nAlso: 'Das Flugzeug hebt ab' = the plane lifts off.

A: I need to withdraw money.
B: Where is the nearest ATM?
A: Around the corner. I will quickly withdraw 100 euros.
B: Good, I will wait here.
Money leaves the account; the plane leaves the ground. Same verb. Separable: 'Ich hebe 100 Euro ab.' The ab- prefix means away/off.
Separable: ab- (off/down) + nehmen (to take).\nThree meanings, all about taking something away.

A: I would like to lose five kilos.
B: Then you need to eat less.
A: Or exercise more.
B: Best to do both!
Three meanings, all about taking something away. 'Fünf Kilo abnehmen' (to lose five kilos). 'Den Hörer abnehmen' (to pick up the receiver). 'Die Kosten nehmen ab' (Costs are decreasing).
The opposite of abnehmen.\nZu- (to/toward) + nehmen = to take on more.

A: In winter I always gain weight.
B: How much?
A: About two kilos.
B: That is not much. In spring you will lose it again.
What goes up (zunehmen) can come down (abnehmen). 'Die Kosten nehmen zu' (Costs are increasing). The opposite twins of German weight and finance talk.
Reflexive. Er- + kalt = to become cold.\nPerfekt: 'Ich habe mich erkältet.'

A: I caught a cold.
B: Again? Dress more warmly.
A: Yes, it was too cold outside.
B: Drink tea and rest.
The verb form of Erkältung. Reflexive: 'sich erkälten.' The Perfekt uses haben because it is reflexive. 'Ich habe mich erkältet' (I caught a cold).
Reflexive. Er- + holen (to fetch).\nYou fetch yourself back from illness.

A: Have you recovered from the flu?
B: Yes, I have recovered well.
A: How long did the recovery take?
B: About a week.
Beautiful metaphor: recovery is self-retrieval. You fetch yourself back from illness. 'Sich von einer Operation erholen' (to recover from an operation).
From erholen + -ung.\n'Erholungsurlaub' = recovery vacation. Kur may be prescribed.

A: You need rest.
B: Yes, I am exhausted.
A: Take a few days of rest.
B: The doctor prescribed me a health retreat.
In Germany, your doctor can prescribe a Kur (health retreat) for Erholung. The Krankenkasse may pay. Recovery is a medical right, not a luxury.
Three meanings in one verb.\n'Geschätzt' = approximately.

A: I estimate the costs at 500 euros.
B: That is a rough estimate.
A: Yes, but I estimate it will not be more.
B: I appreciate your honesty.
Three meanings in one verb. 'Die Kosten schätzen' (to estimate costs). 'Ich schätze deine Hilfe' (I appreciate your help). A word of judgment and gratitude.
Ver- + teilen (to share/divide). Inseparable.\n'Die Kosten werden verteilt.'

A: Who distributes the medicine?
B: The caregiver distributes them.
A: How are the costs distributed?
B: Equally among all insured persons.
The system distributes everything. 'Medikamente verteilen' (to distribute medicine). 'Die Kosten werden verteilt' (The costs are distributed). Inseparable prefix ver-.
Be- + hindern (to prevent).\n'Menschen mit Behinderung' = people with disabilities.

A: The accident is obstructing traffic.
B: For how much longer?
A: About an hour.
B: Then I will take another route.
'Behindert' as an adjective means 'disabled.' 'Menschen mit Behinderung' (people with disabilities) is the preferred modern phrase.
'Er ist gestürzt' = he fell.\nAlso: 'die Regierung stürzen' = to topple the government.

A: What happened?
B: He fell from the ladder.
A: Is he injured?
B: Yes, he broke his arm.
Falls in medicine, revolutions in politics. Same verb. 'Vom Fahrrad stürzen' (to fall off the bicycle). 'Die Regierung stürzen' (to topple the government).
Austrian German. In Germany: 'Arztpraxis.'\nKnowing both words helps across German-speaking countries.

A: Where is the doctor's office?
B: In Vienna they say Ordination.
A: In Germany they say Arztpraxis.
B: Yes, same thing, different word.
Austrian German. In Germany: 'Arztpraxis.' Regional vocabulary: knowing both words helps across German-speaking countries. Austria, Germany, and Switzerland all speak differently.
From 'befreien' (to free).\n'Von der Zuzahlung befreit' = exempt from co-payment.

A: Are you exempt from the co-payment?
B: Yes, I am exempt.
A: Then you do not have to pay anything extra.
B: Good, I have the exemption card with me.
In the insurance context: certain groups are befreit from paying extra. Low-income patients, children, and chronically ill people may be befreit von der Zuzahlung.
From 'befriedigen' (to satisfy).\nAlso a school grade: 3 = befriedigend.

A: How was the treatment?
B: Satisfactory. Not bad, not great.
A: So grade three?
B: Yes, exactly. Satisfactory.
Also a school grade in Germany (3 = befriedigend). Not great, not terrible. The German grade of adequacy. 'Eine befriedigende Lösung' (a satisfactory solution).
From 'Kraft' (strength/force).\n'Kräftig essen' = to eat heartily.

A: You look strong!
B: Thanks, I train a lot.
A: And do you eat heartily?
B: Yes, a hearty soup for lunch.
Conveys robust, physical strength. 'Eine kräftige Suppe' (a hearty soup). German comfort food is kräftig. From 'Kraft' (strength/force).
Same in most European languages.\n'Die gesetzliche und private Versicherung laufen parallel.'

A: The two systems run in parallel.
B: The public and the private one?
A: Yes, parallel to each other.
B: And which one is better?
In insurance: 'Die gesetzliche und private Versicherung laufen parallel' (public and private insurance run in parallel). Germany's dual healthcare system.
'Fachkräftemangel' = shortage of skilled workers.\n'Mangelware' = scarce commodity.

A: There is a shortage of caregivers.
B: Yes, the caregiver shortage is a big problem.
A: What can be done about it?
B: Better pay and more training positions.
Germany's healthcare system suffers from Pflegermangel (caregiver shortage). 'Fachkräftemangel' (shortage of skilled workers) is a national concern across many sectors.
Bade (bathing) + Wanne (tub).\nHaving a Badewanne instead of just a Dusche is a luxury.

A: Does the apartment have a bathtub?
B: Yes, a large bathtub.
A: Great! I love lying in the bathtub.
B: The bathtub is almost new.
In German apartments, having a Badewanne instead of just a Dusche is a luxury. Insurance covers bath aids for elderly patients. Bathing is healthcare in Germany.
zahlen an + accusative. Krankenkasse is feminine. What is the feminine accusative?
First blank: in + dative, neuter Altenheim. Second blank: consequence conjunction.
The nursing home my grandma lives in is very good. Therefore I visit her often.
